w:r.w:t: It's tricky right now because I need something up there but i still don't really want to talk about what we are up to and where, or who is involved etc (from team members to key suppliers)
So at the moment it's essentially an exercise of checking the box with the minimum of content. We will replace it with harder hitting stuff in 3-6 mos.
I made a survey of all the developer sites out there
and came to the conclusion that we needed relatively few sections.
of typical data center developer websites
Clearly there is a lot of this we can
t do because we don’t have the track record or case studies
to trumpet about yet (completed projects etc)
, so we have to be smart about what we show.
Use the Hercules presentation deck as a guide; I’ve already navigated around most of the tricky waters.
from the below at the moment
t have the time or the creds to do the others
, or we don’t want people knowing where we are and what we have going on yet.
Core points are the issues highlighted in the
Through Skills, Experience, First Class Partners & Processes, and a solutions-drive or ‘Frontier’ mentality.
Here are the typical features and sections you’ll find on a professional data center developer/operator website companies like
These sites are designed to attract hyperscale clients, enterprises, colocation customers, and investors.
In short: the website is essentially a B2B sales brochure that screams
scale, reliability, and future-proofing for AI workloads while never revealing actual customer names or exact availability in public.
from the below at the moment….
Hero section with tagline (e.g., “Powering the World’s Digital Future”)
Generic imagery of data center type stuff for now
Key stats: MW under management, locations, total sq ft, customers served
Interactive world map with pins/filters (by region, campus, availability, power, connectivity)
Campus overviews: total MW, buildings, available whitespace/power, expansion plans
Power development (Grid & Behind The Meter) solutions
Tier classification (Uptime Institute Tier III or IV)
List of networks, ISPs, cloud on-ramps present
Logos of major customers (Fortune 500, hyperscalers, financial, content/CDN)
Short success stories (usually anonymized for hyperscalers)
Company history, years in business, total capacity
Financials (if public) or ownership (private equity backing)
Stock ticker, financial reports, investor presentations
Growth story and pipeline (land bank, MW under development)
Blog or “Insights” section on market trends, AI demand, edge computing, etc.
Open positions (lots of electrical/mechanical engineers, project managers)
forms (mandatory fields: company, required capacity, timeline)
Very heavy on visuals: campus renderings, drone footage, interior photos (rarely real hyperscale halls for security)
Gated content (you have to give email to download specs or capacity lists)
Live availability tools (some have real-time inventory for retail colocation suites)
Heavy emphasis on AI-ready facilities (50–150 kW racks, liquid cooling readiness)
Land bank and power pipeline disclosures
